Chapter 20
Carboxylic Acid Derivatives: Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution
©arlindo71/Getty Images
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Carboxylic Acid Derivatives
Carboxylic acid derivatives contain a carbonyl group bound to an electronegative heteroatom
CADs are generated via nucleophilic acyl substitution, where X is a good leaving group (halide, etc.)

Section 20.1
NOMENCLATURE OF CARBOXYLIC ACID DERIVATIVES

Naming Acid Chlorides
To generate the name of the acyl group, replace –ic acid in the parent carboxylic acid with –yl
Add chloride after the name of the acyl group (functional class nomenclature)

Naming Acid Anhydrides
If the two acyl groups are the same, acid in the parent carboxylic acid is replaced by anhydride
If the two acyl groups differ, names of the corresponding acids are listed in alphabetical order
Naming Esters

Naming Amides
Replace –oic acid in the parent acid with –amide
Substituents listed in alphabetical order
Substituents on nitrogen are denoted with the locant N -

Naming Nitriles
Replace the -ic acid or -oic acid ending of the corresponding carboxylic acid with -onitrile.
Alternatively, they are sometimes given functional class IUPAC names as alkyl cyanides.
The suffix -carbonitrile is used when a -CN group is attached to ak ring.

Section 20.2
STRUCTURE AND REACTIVITY OF CARBOXYLIC ACID DERIVATIVES

General Structural Ideas
Carboxylic acid derivatives have a potential leaving group bound to the carbonyl carbon
The electronegativity of the heteroatom determines the quality of the leaving group and reactivity
Reactivity: acyl chlorides > anhydrides > esters > amides
Resonance forms show the importance of electron donation from the heteroatom in stabilizing the carbonyl group
As X becomes more electronegative, stabilization becomes weaker
Structure and Reactivity

Anhydrides versus Esters
Esters are more stable and less reactive than anhydrides
Both involve oxygen donating a pair of electrons to the carbonyl group
However, the central anhydride oxygen is “split” between two carbonyl groups

Amides
N-to-C electron donating is very strong in amides
The C–N bond has considerable double bond character, including a non-negligible barrier to rotation!

Section 20.3
NUCLEOPHILIC ACYL SUBSTITUTION MECHANISMS

Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution
Nucleophilic acyl substitution can take place via several different mechanisms:
Addition-elimination: a tetrahedral intermediate forms after rate-limiting addition; elimination of a leaving group follows
S N 1-like : loss of leaving group to form an acylium ion, followed by nucleophilic attack (e.g., Friedel-Crafts acylation)

Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution-1
Nucleophilic acyl substitution can take place via several different mechanisms:
S N 2-like : (extremely rare) loss of leaving group and nucleophilic attack occur simultaneously
The addition-elimination mechanism is the most common of the three, and occurs under basic or acidic conditions in many cases

Section 20.4
NUCLEOPHILIC ACYL SUBSTITUTION IN ACYL CHLORIDES

Substitution of Acyl Chlorides
Acyl chlorides can be readily converted into any other carboxylic acid derivative (or RCO 2 H itself!)
Rates are faster than substitution of related alkyl halides
Pyridine is often used as a catalyst to make RCOCl more electrophilic and “mop up” hydrochloric acid product

Substitution of Acyl Chlorides-1
TABLE 20.1 Conversion of Acyl Chlorides to Other Carboxylic Acid Derivatives
Reaction (section) and comments General equation and specific example
Reaction with carboxylic acids (see Section 20.4) Acyl chlorides react with carboxylic acids to yield acid anhydrides. When this reaction is used for preparative purposes , pyridine is normally added. Pyridine is a catalyst for the reaction and also acts as a base to neutralize the hydrogen chloride that is formed .
Reaction with alcohols ( see Section 16.8) Acyl chlorides react with alcohols to form esters . The reaction is typically carried out in the presence of pyridine .

Substitution of Acyl Chlorides-2
Reaction with ammonia and amines (see Section 20.12) Acyl chlorides react with ammonia and amines to form amides. A base such as sodium hydroxide is normally added to react with the hydrogen chloride produced .
Hydrolysis (see Section 20.4) Acyl chlorides react with water to yield carboxylic acids. In base, the acid is converted to its carboxylate salt. The reaction has little preparative value because the acyl chloride is nearly always prepared from the carboxylic acid itself.

Section 20.5
NUCLEOPHILIC ACYL SUBSTITUTION IN ACID ANHYDRIDES

Substitution of Anhydrides
Anhydrides may be readily converted to esters, amides, or acids, but not acid chlorides
One of the carbonyl groups ends up in an acid; the other in a carboxylic acid derivative of the nucleophile
Substitution of unsymmetrical anhydrides is…complicated
Acid or base catalysis may be involved

Substitution of Anhydrides-1
TABLE 20.2 Conversion of Acid Anhydrides to Other Carboxylic Acid Derivatives
Reaction (section) and comments
General equation and specific example
Reaction with alcohols (see Section 16.8) Acid anhydrides react with alcohols to form esters. The reaction may be carried out in the presence of pyridine or it may be catalyzed by acids. Only one acyl group of an anhydride is incorporated into the ester; the other becomes the acyl group of a carboxylic acid.
Reaction with ammonia and amines (see Section 20.12} Acid anhydrides react with ammonia and amines to form amides. Only one acyl group of an anhydride is incorporated into the amide; the other becomes the acyl group of the amine salt of the carboxylic acid .

Section 20.6
PHYSICAL PROPERTIES AND SOURCES OF ESTERS

Physical Properties of Esters
Esters are moderately polar and experience dipole-dipole forces
Boiling and melting points are lower than similar alcohols due to lack of hydrogen bonding
Esters can act as hydrogen bond acceptors ; they are somewhat water soluble

Sources of Esters
Esters occur in many contexts in nature
Flavor molecules
Signaling molecules
Esters of fatty acids ( triglycerides , phospholipids )

Preparation of Esters
Esters may be prepared from:
Carboxylic acids and alcohols, with acid catalyst
Acid chlorides and alcohols, with pyridine as catalyst
Acid anhydrides and alcohols, with acid or base catalyst
Ketones via Baeyer- Villiger oxidation
